Welcome back to Wall Power. I=E2=80=99m Marion Maneker. In
tonight=E2=80=99s issue, Julie Davich has a midseason sales update on the p=
ostwar and contemporary art offerings at Sotheby=E2=80=99s, Christie=E2=80=
=99s, and Phillips in New York. Plus, a gallery report on Michelangelo Pist=
oletto at L=C3=A9vy Gorvy Dayan, and Stephanie Shih at Alexander Berggruen.

=C2=A0

Before I toss the keys to the newsletter over to her, I want to do a quick =
follow-up on
Los Angeles, where I caught a few more gallery shows on my way out of town.=
 (I=E2=80=99ll share at least one of them with you on Tuesday.) While I was=
 in the tent at Frieze on Thursday, I ran into Pace=E2=80=99s Marc Glimcher=
 and the gallery=E2=80=99s president, Samanthe Rubell, who commented that a=
rt fairs had taken on a new dynamic, with more and more of the sales coming=
 through at the end of day two.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

True
to their word, I got these day two sales from them, after we sent out Frida=
y=E2=80=99s newsletter:

* A 1966 sculpture by Robert Indiana for $600,000.
* A new ceramic sculpture by Arlene Shechet for $100,000.

* Michelangelo

cond bite at the apple,=E2=80=9D said L=C3=A9vy Gorvy Dayan senior partner =
Xan Serafin of the gallery=E2=80=99s current show of works by Michelangelo =
Pistoletto=E2=80=94especially since the artist is now 91. In the fall of 20=
20, when the gallery staged its first exhibition dedicated to the northern =
Italian artist, almost nobody saw it due to the pandemic. So they mounted a=
 second show, Michelangelo Pistoletto: To Step
Beyond, in collaboration with Galleria Continua. It=E2=80=99s on view in th=
eir New York location through the end of March.

Pistoletto=E2=80=99s work can=E2=80=99t be easily categorized, though he=E2=
=80=99s sometimes referred to as an =E2=80=9Cobject and action=E2=80=9D art=
ist, since he=E2=80=99s known for both assemblages and performances. He exp=
lores themes of reflection, consumption, and technology, and is most closel=
y associated with the Mirror Paintings he=E2=80=99s been making for more th=
an six decades (though technically they are
screenprints, except for the earliest examples). The reflective surface mak=
es the viewer a participant in the work just by looking at it. Some show pe=
ople in various configurations and poses; others feature objects, like a la=
dder or a curtain. When Pistoletto started making them in 1962, they brough=
t him almost immediate international commercial success, thanks to legendar=
y dealer Ileana Sonnabend. Based on Pistoletto=E2=80=99s auction record, hi=
s 1960s figural Mirror Paintings
are by far the most desired by collectors.

After the nonagenarian Pistoletto traveled to New York to oversee the insta=

veloping an on-site hotel there, scheduled to open next summer, to add to t=

cial Times, as a way to =E2=80=9Ctransform the visual infinity that is in m=

mirror paintings.=E2=80=9D

ebys.com/en/buy/auction/2025/contemporary-curated/pastel?utm_campaign=3DWal=
l+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F23%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIB=
ERS+%282%2F23%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&ut=
m_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ), estimated at $200,000, and a striking citron=
-yellow Roni Horn glass
sculpture ( https://www.sothebys.com/en/buy/auction/2025/contemporary-curat=
ed/untitled-he-is-remembered-for-a-remark-he-didnt?utm_campaign=3DWall+Powe=
r+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F23%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2=
82%2F23%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=
=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ), estimated at $400,000. After a blue version of the=
 sculpture sold at Christie=E2=80=99s Evening Sale this past November for $=
1.8 million, Sotheby=E2=80=99s head of sale, Haleigh Stoddard, told me the =
sculpture was =E2=80=9Cat the top of our sourcing list.=E2=80=9D

Sotheby=E2=80=99s is also offering a
single-owner collection ( https://www.sothebys.com/en/auction-catalogue/202=
5/contemporary-curated-n11780/the-infinite-passion-of-life-works-from-the-i=
talian-midcentury?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F23%2F25%29=
&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F23%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demai=
l_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) of nine w=
orks by midcentury Italian artists not usually offered in the Contemporary =
Curated context, estimated at $50,000 to $350,000 each. And last but not le=
ast, there=E2=80=99s a dynamic 2022
painting ( https://www.sothebys.com/en/buy/auction/2025/contemporary-curate=
d/a-garbage-pail-for-my-sickest-feelings?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCR=
IBERS+%282%2F23%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F23%2F=
25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c6061=
7da42abd12a ) estimated at $30,000, by Austyn Weiner, who is about to have =
a small solo presentation at L=C3=A9vy Gorvy Dayan.

=E2=80=8A

Endnotes=E2=80=A6
-----------

There were some great buying opportunities last week at Heritage Auctions=
=E2=80=99
Contemporary Art Within sale. A little floral still life painting by the la=
te Betty Parsons, who has a show up right now at Alexander Gray Associates,=
 sold for $6,000. Four watercolors by the late Paul Jenkins, who has a show=
 opening at Timothy Taylor Gallery in New York next week, sold for between =
$3,750 and $5,500 each. Lastly, three paintings by the late Robert Natkin, =
who is repped by Sundaram Tagore Gallery, sold for $17,500,
$11,250, and $8,750, respectively. (There=E2=80=99s also a Natkin painting =
( https://www.christies.com/en/lot/lot-6522900?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+=
SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F23%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2=
F23%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df=
6c60617da42abd12a ) from the Horowitz collection in Christie=E2=80=99s Post=
-War to Present sale this week, estimated at $3,000.)

=C2=A0

The four works by Gertrude Abercrombie that we
told ( https://puck.news/inside-the-growing-market-for-gertrude-abercrombie=
s-art/?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F23%2F25%29&utm_conten=
t=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F23%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&ut=
m_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) you about in Wright =
and Toomey & Co.=E2=80=99s Elevated: Art Via Chicago sale all exceeded esti=
mates. Owl with Eggs sold for $228,600, almost four times its estimate of $=
60,000. Chicken with Eggs sold for $222,500, more than three times its esti=
mate of $70,000. The smaller, more atypical Keyhole and Eye, estimated at $=
40,000,
sold for $152,400. Lastly, her petite terra-cotta nude, which had no market=
 precedent and was estimated at $30,000, sold for $57,150. More Abercrombie=
 works are coming up at Freeman=E2=80=99s Hindman in May.

=C2=A0

Rago/Wright=E2=80=99s Postwar & Contemporary Ceramics sale that we also tol=
d ( https://puck.news/newsletter_content/gstaad-deal-flow-advisory-economic=
s-ceramicspalooza-3/?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F23%2F25=
%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F23%2F25%29&utm_medium=3De=
mail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) you ab=
out outperformed expectations last week. The monumental sculpture by Jun Ka=
neko sold for $198,000 against an estimate of $60,000; a smaller (but still=
 sizable) one sold for $50,160 against an estimate of $20,000. Peter Voulko=
s=E2=80=99s Bulerias Scratch, estimated at $15,000, sold for $48,260. The f=
ive Kathy Butterly works from the Kalodner collection all sold above their =
estimates. The 17 works by
Toshiko Takaezu all found buyers; the top price of $35,560 was for a Tall C=
losed Form, estimated at $15,000.

One final endnote=E2=80=A6: Larry Gagosian appeared on CBS News Sunday Morn=
ing today in a segment ( https://www.cbsnews.com/amp/news/gallery-owner-lar=
ry-gagosian-on-the-art-worlds-blood-sport/?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBS=
CRIBERS+%282%2F23%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F23%=
2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60=
617da42abd12a ) that was actually filmed last fall, during his
Basquiat exhibition. It repeated a lot of content from Patrick Radden-Keefe=
=E2=80=99s New Yorker profile ( https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2023/07/=
31/larry-gagosian-profile?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F23=
%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F23%2F25%29&utm_mediu=
m=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ). =
There=E2=80=99s been much discussion in the media and the art world about G=
agosian=E2=80=99s succession plans, since he doesn=E2=80=99t have kids. Whe=
n asked by CBS correspondent Anthony Mason whether there could ever be a Ga=
gosian empire without
Gagosian, he answered, =E2=80=9CI don=E2=80=99t know; that=E2=80=99s the qu=
estion. I don=E2=80=99t want to think it=E2=80=99s an impossibility. I thin=
k it=E2=80=99s a serious challenge. But I=E2=80=99m not ready to pass the r=
eins. I enjoy it too much.=E2=80=9D Mason followed up, =E2=80=9CAre you eve=
r going to be, do you think?=E2=80=9D And Gagosian answered, =E2=80=9CNo.=
=E2=80=9D

=E2=80=8A

Thanks, Julie. This was great. I=E2=80=99ll be back on Tuesday.

<em>Pistoletto at L=C3=A9vy Gorvy Dayan</em>: =E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s rare to=

 now 91. In the fall of 2020, when the gallery staged its first exhibition =
dedicated to the northern Italian artist, almost nobody saw it due to the p=
andemic. So they mounted a second show, <em>Michelangelo Pistoletto: To Ste=
p
Beyond</em>, in collaboration with Galleria Continua. It=E2=80=99s on view =
in their New York location through the end of March.<br/><br/>Pistoletto=E2=
=80=99s work can=E2=80=99t be easily categorized, though he=E2=80=99s somet=
imes referred to as an =E2=80=9Cobject and action=E2=80=9D artist, since he=
=E2=80=99s known for both assemblages and performances. He explores themes =
of reflection, consumption, and technology, and is most closely associated =
with the <em>Mirror Paintings </em>he=E2=80=99s been making for more than s=
ix decades (though technically they are
screenprints, except for the earliest examples). The reflective surface mak=
es the viewer a participant in the work just by looking at it. Some show pe=
ople in various configurations and poses; others feature objects, like a la=
dder or a curtain. When Pistoletto started making them in 1962, they brough=
t him almost immediate international commercial success, thanks to legendar=
y dealer <strong>Ileana Sonnabend</strong>. Based on Pistoletto=E2=80=99s a=
uction record, his 1960s figural <em>Mirror Paintings</em>
are by far the most desired by collectors.=C2=A0</li></ul></div>
